coredata.dc:title: Electrochemical DNA biosensor for HPV-16 detection based on novel carbon quantum dots/APTES composite nanofilm
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Yu, Junya
Dong, Cheng
Yang, Yuxing
Yu, Siming
Chen, Tianlan
coredata.dc:description: Detection of high-risk human papillomavirus (HR-HPV) is critical for early warning and accurate screening of cervical disease in women. Electrochemical biosensors offer a promising analytical strategy that can circumvent the structural complexity of traditional optical components found in current bioassays. In this work, an innovative electrochemical biosensor for HPV-16 detection was developed, utilizing advanced nanomaterial technology and optimized DNA probe grafting processes. The self-assembled composite nanofilm was optimized by conducting an orthogonal analysis. The nanofilm exploited the unique advantages and outstanding performance of (3-aminopropyl)triethoxysilane (APTES) and carbon quantum dots (CQDs), which act as a “molecular bridge” between the biosensing interface and the electrode substrate. The electroactive molecule [Ru(NH3)6]3+ facilitated the highly sensitive signaling of the target DNA by interacting with the double-stranded DNA. Under optimal conditions, the developed electrochemical DNA biosensor exhibited high sensitivity in detecting HPV-16, with a limit of detection (LOD) of 0.73 fM, which showed exceptional potential to be used in widespread point-of-care (PoC) screening programs, opening up new opportunities for various applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Human papilloma virus 16
Electrochemical DNA biosensor
Carbon quantum dots
Amino silane
Hairpin structure probe
Composite nanofilm

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Orthogonal analysis of the modification process. Cyclic voltammetry responses of (A) the orthogonal samples of No.1 to No.16, and (B) a comparison of the samples modified under optimal process conditions with the sample No.7, in a 5 mM [Fe(CN)6]3-/4- redox system on CQDs / APTES / Au electrodes.

Characterization performance of modified electrode with a composite nanofilm under optimal process conditions. (A) SEM、(B) CA images, (C) CV and DPV electrochemical responses, and (D) the
I
p
-
v
1 / 2
fitting curves at varying modified electrodes: (a) bare, (b) APTES/Au, and (c) CQDs/APTES/Au, in a 5 mM [Fe(CN)6]3-/4- redox system.

Feasibility investigation of the E-DNA biosensor for HPV-16 detection. (A) The CV response of each assembly step of (I) bare, (II) CQDs/APTES/Au, (III) HSP/CQDs/APTES/Au, (IV) MCH/HSP/CQDs/APTES/Au and (V) TD/MCH/HSP/CQDs/APTES/Au modified biosensors, All measurements were performed in the 0.1 M KCL solution containing 5 mM [Fe(CN)6]3-/4-. (B) The DPV response of each assembly step of (I) bare, (II) CQDs/APTES/Au, (III) HSP/CQDs/APTES/Au, (IV) MCH/HSP/CQDs/APTES/Au, (V) TD/MCH/HSP/CQDs/APTES/Au, (VI) TD/HSP/CQDs/APTES/Au and (VII) TD/MCH /CQDs/APTES/Au modified biosensors, in 10 mM PBS (pH-7.2, 100 mM NaCl) containing 1 mM [Ru(NH3)6]3+.

Optimization of the recognition layer of the E-DNA biosensor. Effects of (A) probe concentration, (B) MCH concentration, and (C) MCH incubation time on the influence of DPV peak gain, error bars = SD (n = 3).

Analyzing the performance of the E-DNA biosensor for HPV-16 detection. (A) DPV responses of the proposed biosensor with various TD concentrations for HPV-16 detection, and (B) the correlation between DPV peak current ΔI and TD concentrations, obtained by an inserted calibration with the logarithm of the TD concentrations, error bars = SD (n = 3). (C) Selectivity of detection for various DNA sequences with base mismatches, and (D) stability of monitoring for up to 15 days at 5-day intervals, with the TD concentration at 100 nM and other sample concentrations at 1 μM, error bars = SD (n = 3).
Schematic representation of the proposed electrochemical DNA biosensor.
Comparison of analysis performance for HPV-16 electrochemical biosensors.

head.ce:title: Electrochemical DNA biosensor for HPV-16 detection based on novel carbon quantum dots/APTES composite nanofilm

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Biomedical Engineering, Jinan University, Guangzhou 510632, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Biomedical Engineering, Jinan University, Guangzhou 510632, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Intelligent Systems Science and Engineering/JNU-Industry School of Artificial Intelligence, Jinan University, Zhuhai 519000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Intelligent Systems Science and Engineering/JNU-Industry School of Artificial Intelligence

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Intelligent Systems Science and Engineering/JNU-Industry School of Artificial Intelligence, Jinan University, Zhuhai 519000, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to the paper.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The electrochemical biosensor consists of an innovative composite nanofilm and an ingeniously designed HSP. The nanofilm is composed of a dense molecular network structure with CQDs as the primary electroactive sensitizers, and APTES was used as a mediator to overcome the challenge of nanoparticle agglomeration. It is worth mentioning that the construction process involved using an orthogonal test to explore the appropriate amount of CQDs nanomaterial and to estimate the optimal process conditions for the SAM on the electrode surface. The stem-loop structure of HSP was found to be an effective strategy to minimize interchain interference and mitigate steric hindrance due to its higher molecular rigidity and fewer exposed bases, resulting in a more homogeneous and organized assembly on the composite nanofilm. During the electrochemical determination, the electroactive molecules Ru(NH3)6Cl3 interacted through a series of pores and were firmly embedded in the long-chain hybrid structure, enabling the quantitative analysis of TD by the electrochemical response signal.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Schematic representation of the proposed electrochemical DNA biosensor.

Detection of high-risk human papillomavirus (HR-HPV) is critical for early warning and accurate screening of cervical disease in women. Electrochemical biosensors offer a promising analytical strategy that can circumvent the structural complexity of traditional optical components found in current bioassays. In this work, an innovative electrochemical biosensor for HPV-16 detection was developed, utilizing advanced nanomaterial technology and optimized DNA probe grafting processes. The self-assembled composite nanofilm was optimized by conducting an orthogonal analysis. The nanofilm exploited the unique advantages and outstanding performance of (3-aminopropyl)triethoxysilane (APTES) and carbon quantum dots (CQDs), which act as a “molecular bridge” between the biosensing interface and the electrode substrate. The electroactive molecule [Ru(NH3)6]3+ facilitated the highly sensitive signaling of the target DNA by interacting with the double-stranded DNA. Under optimal conditions, the developed electrochemical DNA biosensor exhibited high sensitivity in detecting HPV-16, with a limit of detection (LOD) of 0.73 fM, which showed exceptional potential to be used in widespread point-of-care (PoC) screening programs, opening up new opportunities for various applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Cervical cancer (CC), a malignant tumor found in the squamous or glandular epithelium of the cervix, stands as the fourth most common cancer among women worldwide [1], posing a significant threat to women’s health globally [2]. The World Health Organization has adopted a global strategy for cervical cancer elimination and emphasized the importance of quick and accurate screening programs. In turn, the preferred screening method, as explicitly highlighted, is based on detecting high-risk strains of the human papillomavirus (HPV) [3]. Walboomers' research in 1999 found that 99.7 % of invasive cervical cancers (ICC) were associated with high-risk HPV (HR-HPV) [4], and subsequent epidemiologic surveys and research data underscore persistent HPV infection as a definitive causative factor for cervical cancer [5]. In particular, HPV-16 is responsible for approximately 93–100 % of severe cervical precancerous lesions (CIN) [6]. Therefore, quantification of HPV-16 is recommended for prevention, screening, and diagnosis of cervical lesions. This is of variable importance for early warning and etiologic diagnosis in patients with normal or mildly abnormal cytology.

The most commonly applied techniques for HPV-DNA detection include nucleic acids amplification assays (e.g., Cobas 4800), enzyme-linked signal amplification assays (e.g., Cervista HPV), and the widely accepted “gold standard” Qiagen Hybrid Capture (HC II) [7], which are typically used in large hospital laboratories [8]. However, these techniques are associated with high cost and time-consumption, which does not align with the trend of self-sampling in the population but also results in higher loss to follow-up rates among women residing in economically backward and medically underserved regions, further limiting the scale-up of screening programs and continuously increasing the burden of cervical cancer. Therefore, there is an urgent need to identify an efficient, real-time, and cost-effective HPV-DNA screening tool that can shorten the diagnostic cycle and reduce the medical burden [9]. In this context, electrochemical DNA (E-DNA) biosensors have emerged as an attractive option for point-of-care (PoC) based DNA diagnostics due to their rapid, convenient, and cost-effective nature, making them a viable solution for cervical cancer screening in remote and medically underserved areas.

Electrochemical DNA biosensors mimic the electron transfer mechanism present in biological systems, making them a potent tool for the quantitative detection of biochemical analytes [10]. In recent years, there have been innovative advances in the development of electrochemical DNA biosensors for HPV-16 detection, among these are the introduction of loop-mediated isothermal amplification (LAMP) [11], rolling-circle amplification (RCA) [12,13], polymerase amplification (PRA) [14], hybridization chain reaction (HCR) [15], and other signal amplification assays mediated by advanced nanomaterials [16]. While these promising systems aim to improve detection performance, they may introduce background noise and complexity that can be difficult to suppress. As a result, the reliability and reproducibility of electrochemical DNA sensors may be compromised [17]. It is widely recognized that the hybridization, recognition, and conversion of a target gene sequence into electrochemical signals depend on the electrode interface and its modifiers [10]. However, the sensitivity and stability of the predetermined functionality can be affected by the electrode interfaces, which have not received much attention [18]. The application of self-assembled monolayers (SAMs) can ensure the homogeneity of the components and provide specific reactive groups at the electrode interface [19], taking into account the diversity of electrode types and the variability of immobilization conditions. These SAM systems are favored for their ease and controllability of film formation, as well as their ability to precisely regulate chemical and electronic properties at the molecular level. They provide stability and reproducibility for the preparation of chemical and biochemical sensors and are important for the development of biosensors with specific recognition properties and the advancement of other biochemical applications. Meanwhile, the introduction of nanomaterials compensates for the inherent instability of biological structures, and the optimization of receptor molecule packing densities through the use of advanced nanomaterials is highly anticipated.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), serendipitously discovered by Xu in 2004, are emerging zero-dimensional nanomaterials with a particle size of 1–10 nm [20]. Due to their abundant preparation sources, specific surface area, multiple active sites, electroactivity, and biocompatibility [21], CQDs not only facilitate electron conduction and reaction rates at the sensing interface but also enhance the stability in biomolecules without any supporting medium [22]. CQDs have intriguing properties that make them candidates in the areas of biosensing or bioimaging applications [23,24]. In addition, in order to improve the sufficient adsorption and stable presence of CQDs on the electrode interface, here they were dispersed in and coated with the amino-terminated silane agent (3-aminopropyl)triethoxysilane (APTES). APTES, a hybrid bifunctional compound with silicon as the central atom, has two different reactive groups that form “molecular bridges” at the polar-nonpolar interfaces [25]. In fact, the introduction of APTES, besides overcoming agglomeration and promoting the monodisperse state of CQDs, especially enhances the affinal binding to the electrode interface via hydrolysis of the alkoxy groups [26].

In this study, we proposed the innovation of applying a dual-modification strategy of silane coupling agents and carbon-based nanomaterials at the electrode sensing interface, adding new elements to economize and sensitize E-DNA biosensing. Specifically, the optimal modification process conditions from the orthogonal tests were used for the SAMs, which served as a functional “molecular bridge” between the biological recognition interface and the electrode substrate verified by several auxiliary characterization methods. As a result, we successfully constructed a novel E-DNA biosensor for the promising HPV-16 detection platform.

Phosphate buffered saline (PBS), tris (hydroxymethyl) aminomethane hydrochloride (Tris-HCl), and acetic acid (CH3COOH) were purchased from Sangon Biotech Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Sodium chloride (NaCl), magnesium chloride (MgCl2), 6-mercapto-1-hexanol (MCH), trichloride hexaammineruthenium (Ru(NH3)6Cl3), potassium ferricyanide (K3Fe(CN)6), potassium ferrocyanide (K4Fe(CN)6), and TE buffer were purchased from Aladdin Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Sulfuric acid (H2SO4) and anhydrous ethanol (C2H6O) were purchased from Guanghua Sci-Tech Co., Ltd. (Guangzhou, China). Potassium hydroxide (KOH) was purchased from Xilong Scientific Co., Ltd. (Guangzhou, China). APTES was purchased from Shuguang Silane Chemical Co., Ltd. (Nanjing, China), and CQDs were obtained from Janus New-Material Co., Ltd. (Nanjing, China). All the chemicals used in the experiments were of analytical grade. Millipore-Q water (18.2MΩ) was used throughout the experiments.

Detection of target DNA (TD) in this study was accomplished through the utilization of HPV-16 long terminal repeat sequences [27]. To capture the TD, a probe with a stem-loop hairpin structure (HSP) was designed. The HSP consisted of a 'stem' with 5 base pairs that acted as a rigid scaffold to maintain an upright posture on the electrode interface, and a 'loop' with 34 unpaired bases that provided binding sites for the TDs. The oligonucleotide sequences listed in Table S1 were simulated and predicted using http://nupack.org. Sangon Biotech Co., Ltd. custom-made the oligonucleotides, which were then centrifuged, diluted, and dissolved in DNA fixation buffer before being refrigerated at 4 °C until use.

Electrochemical measurements were performed on an electrochemical workstation (CH Instruments, Inc., Shanghai) using a three-electrode system consisting of an Au-modified working electrode, a reference electrode (Ag/AgCl electrode), and an auxiliary electrode (Pt electrode). Gaoss Union Co., Ltd. (Wuhan, China) provided the electrode polishing cloth and alumina powder. The high-magnification morphologies of the electrode coatings were evaluated using a scanning electron microscope (TESCAN MIRA LMS, Czech Republic). Simultaneously, the optical contact angle analysis was carried out using an optical contact angle analyzer (DR-500, Shenzhen, China).

Bare electrodes are generally susceptible to adsorption contamination by oxidation products prior to preparation, which can compromise the stability of the cross-linked structure between the composite film and the electrode substrate, directly affecting the electron transport performance. In order to mitigate this issue, ultrasonic pretreatment was performed on the bare electrodes, which used high-frequency ultrasonic vibrations to generate mild impact and peeling forces, effectively removing residual dirt and other contaminants. Following this, we conducted repetitive scanning cleaning in a 50 mM H2SO4 solution, ranging from −0.4 V to + 1.6 V at a rate of 0.1 V/s, until a stable cyclic voltammogram was obtained using a traditional three-electrode system. The electrodes were sequentially treated with anhydrous ethanol and deionized water for 1 min each, followed by drying with a high-power blow dryer in preparation for subsequent experiments.

To prepare a separate or mixed CQDs/APTES silane hydrolyzed solution, the silane reagent was dissolved in a pre-configured mixture of anhydrous ethanol and deionized water in varying volume ratios. After thorough stirring, the pH value was adjusted with an acetic acid/potassium hydroxide solution while maintaining the total volume of the mixture at 100 mL. The varying amounts of CQDs were added, and the mixture was then placed in a constant temperature bath to undergo hydrolysis for a period of time, resulting in the preparation of a composite solution.

The electrode was modified with a composite solution through manual drop-coating. A natural dehydration reaction was then allowed to occur for 10 min. After that, any excess solution adhering to the electrode was blown off, and the electrode was then placed in a drying oven for thermal curing at a high temperature of 100 °C for 15 min. This process resulted in the formation of a composite nanofilm that bonded to the electrode surface, thereby achieving the objective of chemical modification.

To optimize the modified electrode, the silanization pretreatment technique was used for reference, as well as mechanistic studies [28,29]. An L16 orthogonal design with 4 levels and 5 factors was applied to optimize the hydrolysis process of the composite silane solution doped with CQDs, as shown in Table S2. Voltammetric analysis was conducted using the current response of [Fe(CN)6]3-/4- redox reactions at the modified electrode as an index, which provided feedback on the optimal process parameters for the nanofilm. Later on, techniques such as scanning electron microscopy (SEM), contact angle (CA) measurement, and electroactive surface area calculation were applied to comprehensively characterize the overall performance of the nanomaterial-modified electrode under optimal process conditions. Thus, the electrode modification process with predictability and controllability was achieved.

An electrochemical performance evaluation was conducted in an electrolytic cell system, utilizing an electrolyte solution containing 5 mM [Fe(CN)6]3-/4- in 0.1 M KCl supporting electrolyte. The working electrode, which was modified with a composite nanofilm, was evaluated for its electrochemical performance based on cyclic and differential pulse voltammograms obtained from an electrochemical workstation. Each test was initiated with approximately five scans until the curve stabilized, followed by three repeated measurements, with the average data being recorded.

The morphology and microstructure of the composite nanofilm-modified electrode were observed using secondary electron (SE) imaging principles with a SEM. In addition, an optical contact angle analyzer was used to measure the static CA with water droplets on electrodes with different degrees of modification. The average data was collected from measurements made at three points on the same surface.

The electroactive surface area of the different electrode modifications was calculated by using the Randles-Sevcik equation. Cyclic voltammograms were obtained at various scan rates based on electrochemical performance measurements with the same electrolyte solution, and the scan rate was varied from an initial 50 mV/s in increments of 10 mV/s up to 140 mV/s. The redox peak currents were compared by superimposing the curves and then used for further plotting and fitting of corresponding linear curves. These electrochemical performance measurements provide valuable insight into the performance of the modified electrode and its suitability for various applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical biosensor preparation and electrochemical determination
ce:section.ce:para: The fabrication process for the electrochemical biosensor involved preserving the bare electrode that underwent pretreatment. Next, the composite solution was uniformly mixed using the optimal parameters obtained from the orthogonal test, which was then dropped onto the electrode surface and thermally cured to form strong chemical bonds, resulting in a CQDs/APTES/Au electrode. The COOH-HSP was subsequently diluted to 1 μM in a probe fixation buffer containing 1 M NaCl in 10 mM Tris-HCl and then covalently coupled to the CQDs / APTES / Au electrode surface and incubated overnight, followed by rinsing with deionized water to wash off excess probe. The electrode was soaked in 1 mM MCH for 2 h to block unbound specific sites, then rinsed and dried at ambient temperature to obtain a MCH / HSP / CQDs / APTES / Au electrode. For the hybridization step, the designated TD was diluted in a hybridization buffer containing 150 mM NaCl and 20 mM MgCl2 in 10 mM Tris-HCl, and allowed to fully hybridize for 2 h in preparation for the subsequent electrochemical determination.

The electrochemical determination was carried out using a conventional three-electrode cell system, with signal acquisition performed by the electrochemical workstation. Cyclic voltammograms (CV) were obtained by scanning the potential range from −0.3 V to + 0.8 V at a scan rate of 50 mV/s in a 5 mM solution of [Fe(CN)6]3-/4-. Differential pulse voltammograms (DPV) were conducted in a 10 mM PBS solution containing 1 mM Ru(NH3)6Cl3 and 100 mM NaCl, with a potential range of −0.6 V to + 1 V at a pulse amplitude of 50 mV. The standard evaluation parameter for the determination was the signal gain
Δ I ,
Δ I = I -
I 0
, which was defined as the change in the detection current response relative to the background current response under equilibrium conditions.

The electrochemical biosensor consists of an innovative composite nanofilm and an ingeniously designed HSP, as demonstrated in Scheme 1 . The nanofilm is composed of a dense molecular network structure, with CQDs serving as the primary electroactive sensitizers to enhance the biocompatibility and conductivity of the electrode. In addition, APTES was used as a mediator to overcome the challenge of nanoparticle agglomeration, which can promote effective dispersion and wetting of nanoparticles in the inorganic phase. In addition, the introduction of more than highly polar functional groups, such as amino groups, stabilized the bilayer structure at the electrode-biomolecule interface, resulting in increased electroactive surface area and roughness, which ultimately led to faster electron conduction. It is worth mentioning that the construction process involved using an orthogonal test to explore the appropriate amount of CQDs nanomaterial and to estimate the optimal process conditions for the SAM on the electrode surface.

The successful application of HSP had proven to be an effective strategy to minimize interchain interference and mitigate steric hindrance. Furthermore, the stem-loop structure of HSP, due to its higher molecular stiffness and fewer exposed bases, provided a more homogeneous and organized assembly on the composite nanofilm, thus maintaining its integrity in complex biochemical environments. In this proposed biosensor design, TD detection using HSP was facilitated by stacking forces from base-pair complementarity. During the electrochemical determination, the electroactive molecules interacted through a series of pores and were firmly embedded themselves in the relatively rigid, long-chain hybrid structure, enabling the quantitative analysis of TD through the electrochemical response signal.

The cyclic voltammograms of samples No.1 to No.16, which were obtained from an orthogonal assay, showed regular “duck” patterns, demonstrating their electrochemical catalytic activity interacting with redox systems, as evident from Fig. 1 A. Table S3 describes the orthogonal analysis results of the CQDs/APTES nanofilm-modified electrode, in which only four samples, namely No. 1, No. 5, No. 10, and No. 11, exhibited a reduction current response lower than that of the bare electrode. In contrast, the remaining samples had significantly higher responses. Notably, the current peak of sample No.7 emerged as the most prominent in the entire set of orthogonal tests, with a value of 26.04 μA, which was remarkably higher than the approximately 5.29 μA of the bare electrode.

The present study aimed to determine the optimal conditions for the modification process, identified as A4B3C1D3E1. Specifically, to evaluate and assess the reliability of these conditions, we conducted parallel experiments using samples prepared with 7 % (v/v) of APTES silane reagent, 85 % (v/v) of ethanol concentration, pH 4, 125 mg/L of CQDs nanomaterials, and 6 h of hydrolysis time, and compared with the best sample No.7. Upon analyzing the results, as illustrated in Fig. 1B, it was observed that the current peak of the samples prepared under the optimal process conditions was significantly higher than that of the bare electrode, and slightly higher than that of the best sample. This observation confirmed the reliability of the orthogonal test.

The surface morphology of the electrodes with different degrees of modification was examined by SEM, as shown in Fig. 2 A. In particular, APTES was found to form a uniform thin film on the electrode surface, as evidenced by the smooth surface observed in Fig. 2A (b), indicating effective covalent coupling and the absence of less aggregated structures. Upon doping with CQDs, the electrode surface exhibited dispersed particle structures as seen in Fig. 2A (c), resulting in an island-like morphology and a slight increase in surface roughness. Notably, the CQDs/APTES/Au electrode facilitated the diffusion channels of the reaction solution, thereby improving its overall performance.

CA measurements were used to analyze the wettability of the modified electrode. As shown in Fig. 2B, the introduction of CQDs into the composite nanofilm led to a decrease in the CA. This variation primarily depended on the concentration of CQDs and their functional groups, with the abundance of amino and hydroxyl functional groups forming hydrogen bonds with the aqueous solution to increase the hydrophilicity. This indicated that the modification with SAM favored the wettability of the electrode, which increased the stability of the electrode-solution interface and a greater extent of the reaction with the solution.

The modified electrode was electrochemically characterized using a redox indicator, namely [Fe(CN)6]3-/4-, and was conducted at a scan rate of 50 mV/s, as presented in Fig. 2C. The electrode was modified with APTES and CQDs/APTES to investigate the effect of the silane film and nanomaterial doping. The results showed that the reduction peak current of the CQDs/APTES electrode was significantly enhanced compared to the bare electrode. Specifically, the CV reduction peak current increased from 20.79 μA to 27.16 μA. However, the APTES electrode without CQDs showed only a slight shift in the peak potential with no significant change in the peak current intensity. This finding suggested that the silane film alone did not significantly promote the redox reaction. On this basis, the DPV behavior of electrodes modified with APTES films with and without nanomaterial doping was investigated on this basis.

The incorporation of CQDs under optimal process conditions resulted in a significant increase in the electron transfer rate, as evidenced by the small window in Fig. 2C. After excluding the double-layer capacitance effect, the DPV peak current intensity increased from 39.6 μA to 114.5 μA, representing an approximately 189.1 % increase in the Faraday current. This supported the notion that the SAM of electrode functionalization not only achieved controllability by optimizing the main influencing factors, but also allowed to benefit from the synergistic advantages of APTES and CQDs, which maximized the retention of the physicochemical properties of the nanomaterials. The proposed methodology is promising for the development of high-performance electrochemical sensors and devices.

The electrochemical responses of modified electrodes in a [Fe(CN)6]3-/4- redox system were investigated under varying scan rates, as shown in Fig. S1, along with the corresponding
I
pc
-
v
1 / 2
and
I
pa
-
v
1 / 2
fitting curves are shown in Fig. 2D. Despite the change in scan rate, all the electrodes with different modification states exhibited an increase in peak current with increasing scan rate, with
I
pc
/
I
pa
being approximately equal to 1. Notably, all electrodes exhibited symmetrical redox peaks governed by a diffusion-controlled process. The cathodic peak currents (
I
pc
) and anodic peak currents (
I
pa
) of the bare, APTES/Au, and CQDs/APTES/Au electrodes showed a linear correlation with the square root of the scan rate
v
1 / 2
, as determined by linear fitting methods.

Based on the information presented in Table S4 and the application of the Randles-Sevcik equation, as described in the support material. The electroactive area of a 2 mm diameter bare electrode was calculated to be approximately 3.2 mm2, indicating that the electrode pretreatment was in compliance with certain cleanliness specifications. However, after modification with APTES, the surface area decreased by 41 % due to increased thickness and density. Nevertheless, we observed a significant increase in the conductive and electrocatalytic area following doping with CQDs nanomaterials, and the electroactive area of the CQDs/APTES-modified electrode increased from 1.9 mm2 to 2.16 mm2. Since the diffusion layer on the electrode surface had a certain thickness, the area calculated by this method might be slightly smaller than the actual area. In conclusion, the functionalized electrode surface improves the efficiency of electron transfer and provides more active sites, thus providing a highly ordered molecular-level interface for the redox reaction.

The feasibility of the electrochemical biosensor was validated through both CV and DPV for each assembly step. The [Fe(CN)6]3-/4- redox indicator, which characterized the electron transfer capability of the electrodes, was evaluated in the cyclic voltammograms of Fig. 3 A. A significant amperometric response was observed for the CQDs/APTES electrode (curve II) compared to the pretreated bare electrode (curve I). After the HSP grafting (curve III), the introduced negatively charged phosphate backbone and the anion formed the repulsion, which hindered the electron transfer on the composite nanofilm, thus significantly reducing the redox peaks. The electron transfer pathway was further obstructed when a dense monolayer with MCH was formed (curve IV), resulting in a slight decrease in the redox peaks. When the target DNA hybridized with the HSP (curve V), the formation of long-chain DNA assemblies at the sensing interface further impeded mass diffusion and conversion to the electrode surface, resulting in an additional decrease in the redox peaks.

Furthermore, DPV electrochemically characterized the biosensor behavior in [Ru(NH3)6]3+ electrolyte solution. The bare electrode pretreatment program and the electron transfer performance resulted in a relatively significant signal enhancement, as indicated by curve I in Fig. 3B. The high-density film isolated the electron transfer efficiency of [Ru(NH3)6]3+ at the biphasic interface after modifying the CQDs/APTES SAM (curve II), resulting in a weak response signal. As shown by curve III in Fig. 3B, the HSP modification incorporated a small amount of [Ru(NH3)6]3+ into the stem-end double-stranded structure of the HSP, resulting in an increased response signal of 6.9 μA. The response signal decreased slightly after forming a high-impedance system by blocking active sites with MCM (curve IV). When the target DNA hybridized with the HSP (curve V), the efficiency of electron transfer between the [Ru(NH3)6]3+ electrolyte solution and the electrode substrate varied with the hybridization morphology and charge accumulation on the sensing interface, generating a strong response signal of up to 9.6 μA.

In addition, the non-specific adsorption of TD at the sensing interface caused wastage and did not produce the intuitive increase in response when only DNA hybridization occurred in the assay buffer in the absence of an electrode-blocking agent MCH (curve VI). On the other hand, when only the MCM monolayer and the target DNA were present without HSP grafting (curve VII), the free TD was unable to bind in the vicinity of the sensing interface and underwent minimal non-specific adsorption at a few sites, resulting in a near-zero response signal. The CV and DPV characterization results were shown to be in agreement, confirming the feasibility and effectiveness of HPV-16 detection.

E-DNA biosensors are designed to discern the target DNA by primarily evaluating the disparities in electron transfer kinetics that occur before and after the capture probe recognition process. The immobilization of a recognition layer is a crucial step in the construction of the biosensor, which typically incorporates single-stranded DNA (ssDNA) as the receptor and is also constructed using MCH as the electrode-blocking agent (EBA) [30]. In such a case, during the construction process of the recognition layer, specific key parameters, such as the probe concentration, MCH concentration, and MCH incubation time were selectively optimized to improve the performance. These parameters were found to significantly affect the DPV responses, as demonstrated in Fig. S2. The study used a single-factor design, with each experiment being replicated thrice to facilitate error analysis.

The HSP concentration was initially diluted to the desired level using a probe fixation buffer, followed by optimization tests, which revealed a two-stage change pattern, as depicted in Fig. 4 A. In the first stage, as the HSP concentration was gradually increased from 10 pM to 100 nM, the response signal demonstrated a slow upward trend, reaching a maximum of 6.9 μA. In the second stage, as the HSP concentration was further increased from 100 nM to 1 μM, the response signal decreased to 6.7 μA. These results suggest that a low probe concentration could potentially impair target recognition, resulting in a weak detection current, whereas a high concentration could lead to spatial obstruction, affecting fixation efficiency and system responsiveness.

After two hours of incubation in solutions containing varying concentrations of MCH, the electrodes were analyzed to determine the optimal concentration. The results revealed two distinct trends, with 0.5 mM MCH as the turning point, as shown in Fig. 4B. The coverage of the formed monolayer was relatively low as the concentration increased from 0 mM to 0.5 mM due to the limited length of the MCH chain, which failed to achieve an ideal sealing effect. The signal gain decreased until it reached a plateau at 0.5 mM, resulting in an optimal dense sealing effect that reduced the response signal to 2.39 μA. However, when the MCH concentration exceeded 5 mM, the response signal tended to increase rapidly, followed by a rapid decrease. This was attributed to the fact that at high concentrations, MCH replaced the probe, sealing defects on the electrode and blocking non-specific binding sites.

Optimization of the MCH incubation time was carried out using a 0.5 mM MCH solution for varying time periods. Fig. 4C illustrates that the signal gain gradually decreased with increasing incubation time, and an ideal sealing effect was achieved at 60 min. Further increase of the incubation time resulted in a plateau in the changes of the signal gain, indicating the transition of the formed MCH monolayer from a disordered state to a uniformly regular one. In brief, the electrode surface was precisely controlled using CQDs in conjunction with APTES as the key film-forming agent, and 100 nM HSP was covalently bound to the formed SAM, followed by a 60-minute incubation treatment with 0.5 mM MCH. This resulted in an optimal state of receptor layer components at the electrode/solution interface, which enhanced subsequent target recognition and made it highly efficient.

Utilizing the optimized conditions, the biosensor was constructed in a good status to estimate the DPV response for various concentrations of HPV-16. The MCH/HSP/CQDs/APTES/Au electrode exposed the TD within the dynamic range of the standard series concentrations, widely ranging from 10 aM to 10 μM, as shown in Fig. 5 A. The signal conversion was in correspondence with the embedding interaction of [Ru(NH3)6]3+ with the double-stranded hybrid, Fig. 5B shows that the DPV peak response increased in a progressive and proportional manner with the TD concentration, demonstrating an expected concentration effect. Furthermore, the linear fitting curve within the small insertion window of Fig. 5B indicates a satisfactory linear response of the DPV current in the logarithmic range of TD concentrations from 100 fM to 100 nM. The calibration curve versus TD concentrations was determined according to the equation:
Δ I = 0.696 l g
C
T D
+ 8.262
(
R
2
= 0.992 )
. The limit of detection (LOD) of this proposed biosensor was calculated according to the formula:
L O D =
3 σ / S
= 7.346 ×
10
- 16
M
, where σ represents the standard deviation of the blank control, and S represents the slope of the established linear regression equation.

Based on the analysis above, the biosensor presented in this study exhibits high detection sensitivity without the application of super-long DNA nanochain structures or traditional nucleic acid amplification techniques. In comparison with other biosensors used for the diagnosis of HPV-16, as reported in the literature over recent years (Table 1 ), a direct evaluation relying exclusively on the proposed strategy may yield a one-sided perspective. However, the advantage of our strategy lies in its focus on biosensor construction, with in particular the application of an orthogonal experimental design, we optimize a biocompatible intermediate between the sensing receptor and the electrode surface. This optimization process allows for precise control of the experimental conditions, which enhances the high controllability and allows fine-tuning of the electrode surface functionalization. The stability of the silane-based SAM contributes to the long-term performance of the sensor, which is highly resistant to the potential denaturation or desorption of the biomolecular layer that can occur with traditional Au-S bonds, as demonstrated in Fig. S4. Moreover, the incorporation of CQDs nanomaterials not only increases the contact area between the probe molecules and the electrode, but also enhances the immobilization through various interactive forces. In this case, a high-potential biosensor was developed with favorable results in terms of LOD.

Zr-based porphyrinic MOFs (PCN-224); Polyacrylamide (PAM); Dopamine (DA); Electrochemiluminescence (ECL); Indium tin oxide (ITO); Screen-printed carbon eletrodes (SPCE); Cysteamine(Cys); Polypyrrole (PPy); Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS); Molybdenum disulfide (MoS2); Porous reduced graphenen oxide (prGO); Glassy carbon (GC); Graphene-polyaniline (G-PANI); Square-wave voltammetry (SWV); Anthraquinone(AQ); Silica Nanoparticles-methylene blue (SiNPs-MB).

The biosensor constructed under optimal conditions was used to evaluate the specificity in hybridization for different DNA sequences with base mismatches using DPV. Three parallel assays were performed for each group for error analysis. The corresponding DPV response curve is shown in Fig. S3. The obtained results indicate that within the error range, the signal response between the 1MT sequence, the 2MT sequence, the NC sequence, and HPV-18 were similar to that of the blank control group of the probe without a target, as shown in Fig. 5C. The DPV response was most prominent when the target was a fully matched TD due to its strict requirement for base complementary pairing, which was significantly higher than the response to other incompletely matched DNA sequences. These findings highlight the excellent selectivity and capability of the biosensor presented in this study to identify mismatched DNA targets.

In addition, the stability of the biosensor was evaluated by storing it in a refrigerator at 4 °C and measuring it every 5th, 10th, and 15th day, with three parallel tests for each group. According to Fig. 5D, the DPV response signal showed a slight decrease after the 5th day throughout the test period but remained above 84 % of the initial response after the 10th day. These results confirm that the biosensor has long-term storage stability, which can sufficiently meet the detection requirements in practical applications.

E-DNA biosensors are typically subject to significant interference from complex biological actions, such as nuclease degradation or protein adsorption in the assay environment. These interferences can severely compromise the nucleic acid detection response. Therefore, the new biosensor will must demonstrate precision and anti-interference capabilities in complex matrices or practical biological applications. We used the standard addition method by adding known concentrations of TD to serum solutions and performed recovery testing within the detection range to evaluate the performance of the detection techniques in a relatively homogeneous assay platform. Table S5 presents the performance of HPV-16 determination in serum, demonstrating that the recovery rates of HPV-16 in spiked samples ranged from 98.59 % to 101.91 %, with relative standard deviations (RSDs) between 4.86 % and 9.86 %. The results reveal a high degree of agreement between the detection results and the actual concentrations, with relative deviations less than 9.86 %. Accordingly, the biosensor has potential applicability in initial clinical analysis and constitutes a preliminary step in the development of a diagnostic tool.

The rapid and sensitive detection of HPV at the molecule level is of great clinical significance for the prevention and diagnosis of cervical cancer as well as other HPV-related malignancies. Electrochemical biosensors have been validated by numerous researchers as an effective tool for the cost-effective detection of human disease-related gene mutations or DNA sequences. This study aimed to develop a sensitive, simple, and versatile E-DNA biosensor. It was achieved by synergistically utilizing the unique advantages of APTES and CQDs, innovating a composite nanofilm on the electrode interface for improved sensing performance, and optimizing a recognition layer with HSP to enhance the ability to recognize specific sequences. As was demonstrated, the proposed strategy provided a highly sensitive trace detection capability of HPV-16, a common HR-HPV associated with early warning and etiologic diagnosis of cervical cancer, which showed a LOD of 0.73 fM in the range of 100 fM to 100 nM, even without the application of the nucleic acid amplification techniques. The biosensor exhibited exceptional selectivity, stability, and reproducibility, making it appropriate for clinical diagnostic analysis. It could provide valuable insights into the rational regulation of functional nanomaterials and the conformational design of nucleic acid probes.

Junya Yu: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Cheng Dong: Writing – review & editing, Resources, Project administration, Investigation. Yuxing Yang: Validation, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Siming Yu: Writing – review & editing, Resources, Project administration, Investigation. Tianlan Chen: Writing – review & editing, Resources, Project administration, Investigation.
